While Jeremiah looks at the potter’s work, God brings two great truths into his mind. God has authority and power to form and fashion kingdoms and nations as he pleases. He may dispose of us as he thinks fit; and it would be as absurd for us to dispute this as for clay to quarrel with the potter. But he always acts according to fixed rules of justice and goodness. When God is coming against us in judgments, we may be sure it is because of our sins; but sincere turning away from sin will prevent punishment, both for individuals, families, and nations.

Sinners call it liberty to live however they please; whereas for a man to be enslaved by his desires is the worst slavery. They abandoned God for idols. When people are parched with heat and find cooling, refreshing streams, they use them. In these things people will not leave certainty for uncertainty; but Israel left the ancient paths appointed by the Divine law. They did not walk in the highway where they might travel safely, but in a way in which they must stumble: such was the way of idolatry, and such is the way of wickedness. This made their land desolate and themselves miserable. Calamities may be endured if God smiles upon us while we are suffering them; but if he is displeased and refuses his help, we are ruined. Many forget the Lord and his Christ and wander from the ancient paths to walk in ways of their own devising. But what will they do in the day of judgment!

When the prophet called for repentance, instead of obeying the call, the people devised schemes against him. Thus sinners deal with the great Intercessor, crucifying him again and speaking against him on earth, while his blood speaks for them in heaven. But the prophet had done his duty to them; and that will be our rejoicing in a day of evil.
